main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Comprehensive analysis of the causes of the modality gap in contrastive models","Introduction of the term 'contrastive gap' to describe the phenomenon","Fine-tuning CLIP models by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the loss function to minimize the contrastive gap"],"problems":["Understanding the cause of the modality gap in contrastive multi-modal models like CLIP","Determining whether the gap is due to modality, data, architecture, or loss landscape","Improving the representational space for better downstream performance in multi-modal models"],"datasets":["CLIP pretraining dataset (400M image-caption pairs)","Other multi-modal datasets in domains such as medical images, videos, amino acid sequencing, and brain decoding (referenced, but not specified in detail)"],"metrics":["Downstream task performance (image-text retrieval, zero-shot image classification, multi-modal arithmetic)","Size of the contrastive gap (distance between modality embeddings)"],"results":[{"metric":"Downstream task performance (image-text retrieval, zero-shot image classification, multi-modal arithmetic)","value":"Improved after minimizing the contrastive gap via uniformity and alignment terms"},{"metric":"Size of the contrastive gap","value":"Reduced by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the loss function"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that the so-called 'modality gap' is actually a 'contrastive gap' caused by the contrastive loss in high-dimensional spaces, not by modality, data, or architecture artifacts.","Shows that the contrastive gap can be minimized by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the loss function during fine-tuning.","Provides evidence that closing the contrastive gap leads to improved performance on downstream tasks."]}

methods: Comprehensive analysis of the causes of the modality gap in contrastive models
Introduction of the term 'contrastive gap' to describe the phenomenon
Fine-tuning CLIP models by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the loss function to minimize the contrastive gap
problems: Understanding the cause of the modality gap in contrastive multi-modal models like CLIP
Determining whether the gap is due to modality, data, architecture, or loss landscape
Improving the representational space for better downstream performance in multi-modal models
datasets: Other multi-modal datasets in domains such as medical images, videos, amino acid sequencing, and brain decoding (referenced, but not specified in detail)
metrics: Downstream task performance (image-text retrieval, zero-shot image classification, multi-modal arithmetic)
Size of the contrastive gap (distance between modality embeddings)

results.metric: Downstream task performance (image-text retrieval, zero-shot image classification, multi-modal arithmetic)
results.value: Improved after minimizing the contrastive gap via uniformity and alignment terms
Reduced by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the loss function

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that the so-called 'modality gap' is actually a 'contrastive gap' caused by the contrastive loss in high-dimensional spaces, not by modality, data, or architecture artifacts.
Shows that the contrastive gap can be minimized by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the loss function during fine-tuning.
Provides evidence that closing the contrastive gap leads to improved performance on downstream tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Adding uniformity and alignment terms to the CLIP contrastive loss to encourage more uniform and aligned embedding distributions","Fine-tuning CLIP with the modified loss function"],"problems":["Understanding the cause of the modality gap in two-encoder contrastive models like CLIP","Addressing the contrastive gap that arises from the contrastive loss in multi-modal models","Improving the representational space for downstream tasks by closing the contrastive gap"],"datasets":["CLIP's 400M image-caption pairs dataset (implied)","Other multi-modal datasets in domains such as medical images, videos, amino acid sequencing, and brain decoding (referenced, but not specified by name)"],"metrics":["Zero-shot image classification accuracy","Performance on multi-modal embedding arithmetic tasks","Uniformity and alignment of embeddings in latent space"],"results":[{"metric":"Downstream task performance (zero-shot image classification, multi-modal arithmetic)","value":"Improved compared to default CLIP loss after closing the contrastive gap"},{"metric":"Uniformity of embeddings in latent space","value":"Increased with the proposed method"},{"metric":"Alignment of embeddings","value":"Improved with the proposed method"}],"novelty_claims":["The paper redefines the 'modality gap' as a 'contrastive gap' inherent to the two-encoder contrastive loss, not due to previously assumed factors.","It provides evidence that the contrastive gap is caused by low uniformity in the CLIP embedding space, not by the cone effect, mismatched pairs, or insufficient training.","It proposes a simple and effective method to close the contrastive gap by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the CLIP loss.","It demonstrates that closing the contrastive gap leads to better performance on downstream tasks."]}

methods: Adding uniformity and alignment terms to the CLIP contrastive loss to encourage more uniform and aligned embedding distributions
Fine-tuning CLIP with the modified loss function
problems: Understanding the cause of the modality gap in two-encoder contrastive models like CLIP
Addressing the contrastive gap that arises from the contrastive loss in multi-modal models
Improving the representational space for downstream tasks by closing the contrastive gap
datasets: Other multi-modal datasets in domains such as medical images, videos, amino acid sequencing, and brain decoding (referenced, but not specified by name)
metrics: Performance on multi-modal embedding arithmetic tasks
Uniformity and alignment of embeddings in latent space

results.metric: Downstream task performance (zero-shot image classification, multi-modal arithmetic)
results.value: Improved compared to default CLIP loss after closing the contrastive gap

results.metric: Uniformity of embeddings in latent space

novelty_claims: The paper redefines the 'modality gap' as a 'contrastive gap' inherent to the two-encoder contrastive loss, not due to previously assumed factors.
It provides evidence that the contrastive gap is caused by low uniformity in the CLIP embedding space, not by the cone effect, mismatched pairs, or insufficient training.
It proposes a simple and effective method to close the contrastive gap by adding uniformity and alignment terms to the CLIP loss.
It demonstrates that closing the contrastive gap leads to better performance on downstream tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Analysis of contrastive representation learning through the lens of alignment and uniformity on the hypersphere","Proposal of theoretically-motivated, optimizable metrics for alignment and uniformity","Direct optimization of alignment and uniformity metrics as an alternative to contrastive loss"],"problems":["Understanding the properties that make contrastive representation learning effective","Quantifying and optimizing representation quality in terms of alignment and uniformity on the hypersphere","Evaluating whether direct optimization of alignment and uniformity can match or surpass contrastive learning"],"datasets":["STL-10","Standard vision datasets (unspecified)","Standard language datasets (unspecified)"],"metrics":["Alignment metric (proposed)","Uniformity metric (proposed)","Downstream task performance (e.g., linear separability, classification accuracy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Downstream task performance (direct optimization of alignment/uniformity)","value":"Comparable or better than contrastive learning"},{"metric":"Agreement between alignment/uniformity metrics and downstream performance","value":"Strong agreement observed"}],"novelty_claims":["Identification and formalization of alignment and uniformity as key properties optimized by contrastive loss on the hypersphere","Proposal of new, theoretically-motivated, optimizable metrics for alignment and uniformity","Demonstration that direct optimization of these metrics yields representations with comparable or better downstream performance than traditional contrastive learning","Theoretical proof that contrastive loss asymptotically optimizes alignment and uniformity"]}

methods: Analysis of contrastive representation learning through the lens of alignment and uniformity on the hypersphere
Proposal of theoretically-motivated, optimizable metrics for alignment and uniformity
Direct optimization of alignment and uniformity metrics as an alternative to contrastive loss
problems: Understanding the properties that make contrastive representation learning effective
Quantifying and optimizing representation quality in terms of alignment and uniformity on the hypersphere
Evaluating whether direct optimization of alignment and uniformity can match or surpass contrastive learning
metrics: Downstream task performance (e.g., linear separability, classification accuracy)

results.metric: Downstream task performance (direct optimization of alignment/uniformity)
results.value: Comparable or better than contrastive learning

results.metric: Agreement between alignment/uniformity metrics and downstream performance

novelty_claims: Identification and formalization of alignment and uniformity as key properties optimized by contrastive loss on the hypersphere
Proposal of new, theoretically-motivated, optimizable metrics for alignment and uniformity
Demonstration that direct optimization of these metrics yields representations with comparable or better downstream performance than traditional contrastive learning
Theoretical proof that contrastive loss asymptotically optimizes alignment and uniformity

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SimCLR: a simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations","Systematic study of data augmentation composition for contrastive tasks","Use of a learnable nonlinear transformation (projection head) between representation and contrastive loss","Contrastive cross-entropy loss with normalized embeddings and temperature scaling","Training with large batch sizes and longer training durations"],"problems":["Learning effective visual representations without human supervision (self-supervised learning)","Improving the quality of learned representations in contrastive learning frameworks","Reducing reliance on specialized architectures or memory banks in self-supervised learning"],"datasets":["ImageNet ILSVRC-2012","Other natural image classification datasets (10 out of 12 unspecified datasets)"],"metrics":["Top-1 accuracy","Top-5 accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Top-1 accuracy (ImageNet, linear evaluation)","value":"76.5%"},{"metric":"Relative improvement over previous state-of-the-art (Top-1 accuracy)","value":"7%"},{"metric":"Top-5 accuracy (ImageNet, 1% labels, fine-tuned)","value":"85.8%"},{"metric":"Relative improvement over previous state-of-the-art (Top-5 accuracy, 1% labels)","value":"10%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a simple contrastive learning framework (SimCLR) that does not require specialized architectures or memory banks","Systematic study and demonstration of the importance of data augmentation composition in contrastive learning","Introduction of a learnable nonlinear projection head between representation and contrastive loss, improving representation quality","Shows that contrastive learning benefits from larger batch sizes and longer training compared to supervised learning","Achieves state-of-the-art results in self-supervised and semi-supervised learning on ImageNet"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Training a dual-encoder architecture (ALIGN) to align visual and language representations using a contrastive loss on noisy image alt-text pairs","Leveraging a large-scale, noisy dataset of over one billion image alt-text pairs with minimal filtering","Contrastive learning objective (normalized softmax) to align image and text embeddings in a shared latent space"],"problems":["Reducing reliance on expensive, curated, and annotated datasets for visual and vision-language representation learning","Scaling up visual and vision-language representation learning using noisy, large-scale data","Improving transferability and zero-shot capabilities of learned representations"],"datasets":["Noisy dataset of over one billion image alt-text pairs (collected using procedures similar to Conceptual Captions, but with minimal filtering)","ImageNet","VTAB","Flickr30K","MSCOCO"],"metrics":["Top-1 accuracy (ImageNet)","Recall@1 (R@1) for image-text retrieval (Flickr30K, MSCOCO)"],"results":[{"metric":"ImageNet Top-1 Accuracy (zero-shot)","value":"76.4%"},{"metric":"ImageNet Top-1 Accuracy (transfer)","value":"88.64%"},{"metric":"Flickr30K R@1 (zero-shot and fine-tuned)","value":">7% improvement over previous SOTA"},{"metric":"MSCOCO R@1 (zero-shot and fine-tuned)","value":">7% improvement over previous SOTA"}],"novelty_claims":["First to demonstrate that large-scale noisy image-text data (over one billion pairs) can be used to learn state-of-the-art visual and vision-language representations without expensive curation or annotation.","Propose ALIGN, a simple dual-encoder model trained with contrastive loss, that achieves SOTA on both vision and vision-language benchmarks.","Show that scale can compensate for noise in training data, enabling strong zero-shot and transfer performance."]}

methods: Training a dual-encoder architecture (ALIGN) to align visual and language representations using a contrastive loss on noisy image alt-text pairs
Leveraging a large-scale, noisy dataset of over one billion image alt-text pairs with minimal filtering
Contrastive learning objective (normalized softmax) to align image and text embeddings in a shared latent space
problems: Reducing reliance on expensive, curated, and annotated datasets for visual and vision-language representation learning
Scaling up visual and vision-language representation learning using noisy, large-scale data
Improving transferability and zero-shot capabilities of learned representations
datasets: Noisy dataset of over one billion image alt-text pairs (collected using procedures similar to Conceptual Captions, but with minimal filtering)
novelty_claims: First to demonstrate that large-scale noisy image-text data (over one billion pairs) can be used to learn state-of-the-art visual and vision-language representations without expensive curation or annotation.
Propose ALIGN, a simple dual-encoder model trained with contrastive loss, that achieves SOTA on both vision and vision-language benchmarks.
Show that scale can compensate for noise in training data, enabling strong zero-shot and transfer performance.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP): jointly training an image encoder and a text encoder to predict correct (image, text) pairings using a contrastive objective","Pre-training on 400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet","Zero-shot transfer: using the text encoder to synthesize a classifier for new tasks by embedding class names or descriptions"],"problems":["Limited generality and usability of computer vision models trained on fixed, predetermined object categories","Need for large amounts of labeled data to specify new visual concepts","Lack of scalable methods for learning visual representations directly from natural language supervision","Poor zero-shot transfer performance of previous natural language supervised models"],"datasets":["400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet (proprietary dataset for pre-training)","Over 30 existing computer vision datasets for evaluation, including ImageNet (for zero-shot and linear probe evaluation), OCR datasets, action recognition datasets, geo-localization datasets, and fine-grained object classification datasets"],"metrics":["Zero-shot classification accuracy","Linear probe classification accuracy","Transfer performance on downstream tasks","Computational efficiency (training speed)"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (CLIP)","value":"matches ResNet-50 trained on ImageNet (without using any ImageNet training examples)"},{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (Li et al., 2017)","value":"11.5%"},{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (state of the art, Xie et al., 2020)","value":"88.4%"},{"metric":"Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (classic computer vision, Deng et al., 2012)","value":"50%"},{"metric":"Zero-shot transfer efficiency (CLIP vs. image caption baseline)","value":"CLIP is 4x more efficient than bag-of-words baseline; transformer-based language models are 3x slower than BoW baseline"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that large-scale contrastive pre-training on natural language supervision (400M image-text pairs) can produce highly transferable visual models","CLIP enables zero-shot transfer to a wide range of vision tasks using only natural language descriptions, without any dataset-specific training","CLIP achieves competitive or superior performance to fully supervised models on many benchmarks, including matching ResNet-50 on ImageNet in zero-shot setting","CLIP is more computationally efficient than previous approaches and more robust than supervised models with equivalent accuracy","First to close the scale gap in natural language supervised image representation learning, showing that performance scales smoothly with compute and data"]}

methods: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP): jointly training an image encoder and a text encoder to predict correct (image, text) pairings using a contrastive objective
Pre-training on 400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet
Zero-shot transfer: using the text encoder to synthesize a classifier for new tasks by embedding class names or descriptions
problems: Limited generality and usability of computer vision models trained on fixed, predetermined object categories
Need for large amounts of labeled data to specify new visual concepts
Lack of scalable methods for learning visual representations directly from natural language supervision
Poor zero-shot transfer performance of previous natural language supervised models
datasets: 400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet (proprietary dataset for pre-training)

Over 30 existing computer vision datasets for evaluation, including ImageNet (for zero-shot and linear probe evaluation), OCR datasets, action recognition datasets, geo-localization datasets, and fine-grained object classification datasets

results.value: matches ResNet-50 trained on ImageNet (without using any ImageNet training examples)

results.metric: Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (state of the art, Xie et al., 2020)
Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy (classic computer vision, Deng et al., 2012)
Zero-shot transfer efficiency (CLIP vs. image caption baseline)
results.value: CLIP is 4x more efficient than bag-of-words baseline; transformer-based language models are 3x slower than BoW baseline

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that large-scale contrastive pre-training on natural language supervision (400M image-text pairs) can produce highly transferable visual models
CLIP enables zero-shot transfer to a wide range of vision tasks using only natural language descriptions, without any dataset-specific training
CLIP achieves competitive or superior performance to fully supervised models on many benchmarks, including matching ResNet-50 on ImageNet in zero-shot setting
CLIP is more computationally efficient than previous approaches and more robust than supervised models with equivalent accuracy
First to close the scale gap in natural language supervised image representation learning, showing that performance scales smoothly with compute and data

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Systematic empirical analysis of the modality gap in multi-modal models","Theoretical analysis of the cone effect in neural network embeddings","UMAP visualization of embedding spaces","Manipulation of modality gap distance to study downstream effects"],"problems":["Understanding the modality gap in multi-modal contrastive representation learning","Explaining the geometric separation of different modalities in shared embedding spaces","Analyzing the impact of the modality gap on downstream tasks such as zero-shot classification and fairness"],"datasets":["Natural images (e.g., CLIP dataset)","Text data (paired with images)","Videos","Medical images","Amino-acid sequences"],"metrics":["Zero-shot classification accuracy","Fairness metrics (not specified in detail)","Cosine similarity between embeddings"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot classification accuracy (CLIP, after increasing modality gap)","value":"Improved (exact numbers not specified)"},{"metric":"Fairness (CLIP, after increasing modality gap)","value":"Improved (exact numbers not specified)"},{"metric":"Average cosine similarity between embeddings (random initialization)","value":"~0.99 (high separation)"},{"metric":"Average cosine similarity between embeddings (pretrained)","value":"0.56 (vision), 0.47 (text), 0.51 (transformer)"}],"novelty_claims":["First systematic demonstration of the general modality gap phenomenon across a wide range of multi-modal models and data types.","Three-part explanation (inductive bias/cone effect, random initialization, contrastive learning) for the modality gap, supported by theory and experiments.","Demonstration that modifying the modality gap can improve downstream zero-shot performance and fairness.","Mathematical characterization of the contraction mapping (cone effect) induced by deep neural networks, generalizing previous work."]}

methods: Systematic empirical analysis of the modality gap in multi-modal models
Theoretical analysis of the cone effect in neural network embeddings
UMAP visualization of embedding spaces
Manipulation of modality gap distance to study downstream effects
problems: Understanding the modality gap in multi-modal contrastive representation learning

results.metric: Zero-shot classification accuracy (CLIP, after increasing modality gap)

novelty_claims: First systematic demonstration of the general modality gap phenomenon across a wide range of multi-modal models and data types.
Three-part explanation (inductive bias/cone effect, random initialization, contrastive learning) for the modality gap, supported by theory and experiments.
Demonstration that modifying the modality gap can improve downstream zero-shot performance and fairness.
Mathematical characterization of the contraction mapping (cone effect) induced by deep neural networks, generalizing previous work.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Group-Alignment and Global-Uniformity Enhanced Representation Learning for Debiasing Recommendation (AURL)","Group-alignment regularizer in representation space","Global-uniformity regularizer in representation space"],"problems":["Recommendation bias in collaborative filtering due to imbalanced training data","Over-recommendation of popular items","Poor performance for inactive users","Group-discrepancy and global-collapse in user/item representation distributions"],"datasets":["Three real datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Recommendation accuracy (e.g., standard metrics such as Hit Rate, NDCG, Recall, though not explicitly named in the excerpt)","Bias mitigation (no explicit metric name provided)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new framework (AURL) that mitigates recommendation biases from the perspective of representation distribution.","Introduces two novel regularizers: group-alignment and global-uniformity, to address group-discrepancy and global-collapse in representation learning.","The method is applicable to arbitrary CF-based recommendation backbones.","Demonstrates that optimizing both group-alignment and global-uniformity regularization terms can simultaneously mitigate bias and improve recommendation performance."]}

State-of-the-art performance on standard image-text matching benchmarks (MS-COCO and Flickr30K).

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["y-Aware InfoNCE loss (contrastive loss integrating continuous meta-data)","Decomposition of y-Aware InfoNCE into conditional alignment and global uniformity","Proposal of conditional uniformity (repelling only samples with dissimilar meta-data)","Direct optimization of both conditional alignment and conditional uniformity"],"problems":["Learning effective representations from medical images with available meta-data (e.g., age, sex)","Improving contrastive learning by leveraging continuous proxy labels (meta-data)","Addressing the limitations of global uniformity in contrastive learning for data with meta-data","Improving transferability and quality of learned representations in small, heterogeneous, or biased medical datasets"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100","Brain MRI dataset"],"metrics":["Linear evaluation accuracy"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Show that y-Aware InfoNCE loss asymptotically optimizes conditional alignment and global uniformity.","Propose the concept of conditional uniformity, which repels only samples with dissimilar meta-data, as opposed to global uniformity.","Demonstrate that direct optimization of both conditional alignment and conditional uniformity improves learned representations on both natural and medical image datasets."]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Two-branch neural network architectures for image-text matching","Embedding network: learns a shared latent embedding space using a bi-directional ranking loss with novel neighborhood constraints and improved triplet sampling","Similarity network: fuses image and text features via element-wise product and is trained with logistic regression loss to directly predict similarity scores"],"problems":["Image-text matching: measuring semantic similarity between images (or regions) and text (sentences or phrases)","Phrase localization (visual grounding): matching a phrase to relevant image regions","Bi-directional image-sentence retrieval: retrieving relevant sentences given an image query and vice versa"],"datasets":["Flickr30K Entities","Flickr30K","MSCOCO"],"metrics":["Phrase localization accuracy (e.g., Recall@1, Recall@5, etc.)","Image-sentence retrieval accuracy (e.g., Recall@K, median rank)"],"results":[{"metric":"Phrase localization accuracy (Flickr30K Entities)","value":"State-of-the-art (exact numbers not provided in excerpt)"},{"metric":"Image-sentence retrieval accuracy (Flickr30K, MSCOCO)","value":"Near state-of-the-art (exact numbers not provided in excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes two-branch neural network architectures (embedding and similarity networks) for image-text matching tasks.","Introduces a novel neighborhood constraint and improved triplet sampling strategy for the embedding network, leveraging within-modality neighborhood structure.","Systematic investigation of loss functions, feature fusion strategies, and sampling methods for training two-branch networks.","Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on phrase localization and near state-of-the-art results on image-sentence retrieval."]}

methods: Two-branch neural network architectures for image-text matching
Embedding network: learns a shared latent embedding space using a bi-directional ranking loss with novel neighborhood constraints and improved triplet sampling
Similarity network: fuses image and text features via element-wise product and is trained with logistic regression loss to directly predict similarity scores
problems: Image-text matching: measuring semantic similarity between images (or regions) and text (sentences or phrases)
Phrase localization (visual grounding): matching a phrase to relevant image regions
Bi-directional image-sentence retrieval: retrieving relevant sentences given an image query and vice versa
metrics: Phrase localization accuracy (e.g., Recall@1, Recall@5, etc.)
Image-sentence retrieval accuracy (e.g., Recall@K, median rank)

results.value: State-of-the-art (exact numbers not provided in excerpt)
Near state-of-the-art (exact numbers not provided in excerpt)

novelty_claims: Proposes two-branch neural network architectures (embedding and similarity networks) for image-text matching tasks.
Introduces a novel neighborhood constraint and improved triplet sampling strategy for the embedding network, leveraging within-modality neighborhood structure.
Systematic investigation of loss functions, feature fusion strategies, and sampling methods for training two-branch networks.
Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on phrase localization and near state-of-the-art results on image-sentence retrieval.
